title: Flag book
subtitle: Hinged pages that fan horizontally from an accordion spine
excerpt: A flag book carries leaves attached at alternating valleys and peaks of an accordion spine, so the pages fan out in opposite directions when the spine expands. A compact structure, it unfolds into a wide panoramic display, and closes flat as a slim book.
sourceNotes: The flag book is a 20th-century artist's book structure documented in bookbinding and book-arts manuals from the 1980s onward. The construction described follows the standard flag-book method consistent with those sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: A folded paper spine with alternating valley and mountain folds, expandable like a concertina.
An individual leaf attached to the accordion spine of a flag book, projecting outward in alternating directions when the spine expands.

content.text: A flag book works on a simple mechanical principle: an
expands to either side when opened, and the

glued alternately to the valley and peak folds fan in opposite directions. Closed, the book is a narrow block. Opened, it becomes a panorama of facing leaves. The structure suits image sequences, poetry, maps, and botanical studies.

attrs.heading: Before you start
attrs.body: Plan the spine fold-count before cutting. Each accordion section carries one flag. A flag book with 10 flags needs a 10-section accordion. Decide the page and flag dimensions first, then calculate the total spine length.

items.name: Spine paper, 120 to 160 gsm, long grain
items.notes: A strip the height of the finished book and long enough to fold the required number of sections. Each section is twice the spine-depth (typically 20 to 30 mm).

items.name: Flag paper or card, 120 to 200 gsm
items.notes: Cut to the flag width and the spine height. Grain parallel to the spine height.

items.name: Cover boards, 1.5 mm bookbinding board
items.notes: Two boards cut to the finished page dimensions, for front and back covers.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: For attaching flags to the spine folds and boards to the spine ends.

items.name: Craft knife and metal ruler
items.notes: For cutting spine strip and flags accurately.

content.text: Cut the spine strip to the required length. Score and fold alternating valley and mountain folds at equal intervals, each section 25 mm deep. Use the bone folder against a ruler for each score. Press each fold sharply. Check that all sections are equal in depth by stacking the closed accordion and examining the edges.

Cut one flag for each accordion section. Flag width sets how far the pages project when the book is open; 80 to 120 mm works well for A5-height books. The grain direction runs parallel to the spine height (the shorter dimension of the flag). Decorate or print the flags before assembly if the content is planned in advance.

Apply a thin coat of PVA to a 10 mm tab on one short edge of the first flag. Attach it to the first valley fold of the accordion, pressing the tab firmly along the inside of the fold. The flag projects outward from the valley. On the next section (a mountain fold), attach the next flag to the outside face, so it projects in the opposite direction. Continue alternating: valley folds receive flags on their inner face, mountain folds on their outer face. Allow to dry under light weight.

Apply PVA to the outermost face of each end of the accordion. Press the front cover board onto the first accordion section and the back board onto the last. The boards close the accordion and become the front and back covers of the finished book. Press under weight overnight to ensure a flat bond.

Store a flag book closed and flat. The accordion spine can loosen with repeated opening if the flag tabs were not bonded under sufficient weight. If a tab lifts, work fresh PVA in with a fine brush and re-press.

items.symptom: The accordion sections are unequal in width
items.cause: The spine strip was scored by eye rather than measured.
items.fix: Mark every fold line with a ruler and pencil before scoring. A strip of card the width of one section used as a measuring template prevents accumulation errors.

items.symptom: Flags on one side project further than flags on the other
items.cause: Inconsistent flag widths, or tabs attached at different depths.
items.fix: Cut all flags from the same measured template. Align each tab exactly to the fold line before pressing.
